notes.note: Cicero suffered repeated bouts of lippitudo, an inflammation of the eyes, through this spring of 49 BC; it is why he dictates the letter rather than writing in his own hand. The complaint recurs as the explicit reason for brevity in the next letter (8.13).

notes.anchor: Capua already having been put in my charge
notes.note: At the outbreak of war Pompey had assigned Cicero a supervisory command over Capua and the Campanian coast; Cicero here defends his refusal to take it up as prudence rather than cowardice or bad faith.

notes.anchor: the terms of peace had been brought through Lucius Caesar and Lucius Fabatus
notes.note: In late January 49 BC Caesar sent L. Julius Caesar (a young kinsman) and L. Roscius Fabatus to Pompey and the consuls with proposals for a negotiated settlement; the talks came to nothing. Cicero notes that he was careful not to antagonise Caesar while the embassy was in play.

notes.anchor: conferring the consulship and the triumph of a fellow under arms
notes.note: Among the peace terms Pompey was prepared to grant Caesar the consulship and a triumph; Cicero's pointed phrasing stresses that both parties were already armed, so the offer was made under threat rather than in concord.

notes.anchor: he would come to Domitius's aid
notes.note: L. Domitius Ahenobarbus was besieged by Caesar in Corfinium; Cicero had assumed from Pompey's own letter that Pompey would march to relieve him. Pompey did not, and Corfinium surrendered (mid-February 49 BC) with Domitius captured.

notes.anchor: the book of Demetrius of Magnesia on concord
notes.note: Demetrius of Magnesia was a Greek scholar and friend of Atticus; his treatise On Concord (peri homonoias) was topically apt as the Republic split into civil war. Cicero asks Atticus more than once to forward his copy.
